lens.included_activities: Independent and regional tire dealers in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly sell, fit, balance, rotate, inspect, and repair tires for external retail or fleet customers, including locations that also provide adjacent automotive repair.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure product wholesalers, tire manufacturers and retreaders, captive dealership service departments, national-chain corporate stores, non-operating finance vehicles, and shops without transferable customer, workforce, or operating systems.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat consumer and fleet visits produce transaction revenue from tires plus installation, balancing, rotation, repair, alignment, and related service labor; some fleet relationships are account-based, but most consumer work is episodic rather than contracted.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for the broader 441300 group rather than 441340 alone. I classify customer intake, quote preparation, scheduling, follow-up, inventory decisions, bookkeeping, and portions of supervision as AI-exposed, while treating tire changing, driving, stocking, and most mechanical execution as substantially physical; inclusion of auto-parts-only retailers likely overstates clerical and parts-sales exposure for tire dealers.
a.rationale: The broader industry's largest occupations include 117,730 parts salespersons, 74,340 tire repairers/changers, 71,430 light truck drivers, 56,550 retail salespersons, and 46,870 automotive technicians. That mix leaves a meaningful but minority wage-weighted opportunity in conversational, administrative, dispatch, pricing, and diagnostic-support tasks; physical service and delivery cap substitution.
a.change_evidence: A 441340-specific payroll extract by SOC paired with time-and-motion data for service writers, store managers, dispatchers, technicians, and back-office staff, with current automation tagged task by task.
caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 441300 and includes automotive parts retailers.
ORS requirements describe occupations economy-wide, not tire-dealer establishments.
Task exposure is inferred rather than directly measured, and current point-of-sale automation varies widely.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS reports any-business-function AI use for all Retail Trade, not the share of tire-dealer task opportunity implemented over five years. I use current retail adoption near 14% and six-month expected use near 17% as a low starting point, then allow five years for packaged point-of-sale, call handling, scheduling, marketing, and analytics tools to diffuse; small-firm constraints and physical workflow integration bias implementation below software-native sectors.
rho.rationale: Implementation should be feasible for bounded workflows such as appointment intake, estimates, service reminders, inventory recommendations, and documentation, but fragmented systems, product-liability risk, customer trust, training, and limited IT staff prevent full conversion of exposed work.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal adoption and realized-hours studies from tire-dealer point-of-sale vendors, plus interviews with multi-location operators on deployment rates, override frequency, training burden, and headcount avoidance.
caveats: BTOS measures use of any AI function, not implementation depth or labor savings.
Retail Trade is broader and may differ from service-intensive tire dealerships.
Five-year technology performance and vendor integration are uncertain.

e.rationale: A substantial subset of tire dealers has repeat installation and maintenance activity, external customers, local operating teams, and transferable locations. Eligibility is reduced by pure product retail, owner-centric micro-shops, national-chain units, franchises with transfer restrictions, weak records, and businesses whose economics fall outside the normalized EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A 441340 establishment-to-firm census joined to revenue, EBITDA, ownership type, franchise status, service-labor mix, customer concentration, and owner dependence, supplemented by a broker listing audit.
caveats: The supplied firm-count input is missing, so this share cannot establish an absolute eligible-firm count.
NAICS includes both tire-only retail and tire retail combined with automotive repair.
Public transaction listings skew toward smaller and voluntarily marketed businesses.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. business owners, including 22% of employer-business owners and 17% of owners age 55 or older, rather than completed qualifying control transfers among eligible tire dealers. I center near those intentions, trim for failed, postponed, internal, and non-transferable exits, and offset that trim partly with unplanned sales and observed automotive-service deal flow; intention data likely overstates completion, while excluding unplanned transfers understates it.
s5.rationale: Tire and automotive service businesses demonstrably trade, and aging ownership supports supply, but a five-year qualifying-control-transfer probability should remain below a simple accumulation of annual listing activity because many shops close, remain family-held, fail diligence, or sell assets rather than a transferable operation.
s5.change_evidence: Closed-deal data tagged to NAICS 441340 and seller ownership age, including denominator counts, failed listings, family transfers, asset-only sales, and control status over a five-year cohort.
caveats: Gallup covers all industries and reports intentions, not completed transfers.
BizBuySell combines tire-relevant shops with broader auto repair and represents only reported marketplace transactions.
Transfers may be more common among employer firms but less common in the specific EBITDA band.

q.rationale: Operators can retain part of implemented labor savings through faster intake, higher bay utilization, fewer missed calls, tighter purchasing, and lower administrative hiring. Retention is limited because tire prices are transparent, consumers can compare installed quotes, fleets rebid accounts, and competitors can adopt similar tools; service convenience and local capacity make pass-through slower than in a commodity-only sale.
q.change_evidence: Store-level cohorts comparing realized gross profit per work order, labor hours, quote conversion, price changes, and customer churn before and after specific AI deployments, separated between consumer and fleet accounts.
caveats: No source directly measures contractual or competitive pass-through of AI savings in tire retail.
Retention differs between product margin, service labor, national accounts, and local retail work.
The estimate excludes demand-volume effects and implementation failure.

d5.bridge: USTMA measures U.S. tire unit shipments, including original-equipment and replacement channels, rather than constant-quality service demand at eligible dealers. Replacement passenger, light-truck, and truck shipments are the closest physical-demand proxy; shipment growth can overstate dealer service if online sales or alternate channels gain share and understate service if tire complexity or installation intensity rises.
d5.rationale: USTMA projects 338.9 million total U.S. tire shipments in 2026 versus 336.3 million in 2025 and 332.7 million in 2019, with replacement categories individually near flat to modest growth. Tire wear, punctures, and safety replacement persist across powertrains, supporting broadly stable five-year real service quantity with modest upside and channel-share downside.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of replacement tire units and installation/repair work orders by channel, adjusted for vehicle miles traveled, fleet age, EV mix, tire longevity, online fulfillment, and constant-quality service content.
caveats: A one-year industry forecast is not a five-year demand projection.
Shipments include channels and original-equipment tires outside the operating lens.
Units do not directly capture real service intensity or dealer channel share.

o.bridge: BLS ORS measures present job requirements across installation, maintenance, and repair occupations, not the year-five share of tire-dealer quantity requiring an accountable operator. The 88.9% incidence of people-controlled work, virtually absent routine telework, and physical tire-service tasks are used to infer persistence of an operator-run shop; this may understate future self-service in booking and sales but overstate displacement if robotic tire handling diffuses quickly.
o.rationale: Even if intake, quoting, and reminders become self-service, tire inspection, lifting, mounting, balancing, repair, roadworthiness judgment, customer authorization, and exception handling remain tied to physical locations and accountable operators over five years.
o.change_evidence: Adoption, throughput, staffing, and failure-rate data for robotic tire inspection and mounting systems, plus channel forecasts for mobile service, direct-to-consumer installation, and fully self-service tire transactions.
caveats: ORS is occupation-wide and current, not tire-specific or forward-looking.
Operator requirement is distinct from the number of workers needed per transaction.
The upper bound assumes no rapid breakthrough in economical small-shop robotics.

narrative.executive_view: Tire dealers combine durable, physically delivered replacement demand with a meaningful but bounded opportunity to automate the customer and administrative layer. The operating opportunity is strongest in multi-location firms where call handling, scheduling, quoting, purchasing, reminders, and management reporting repeat at scale; it is weaker in owner-run shops and in the technician work itself.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can answer and triage calls, prepare fitment-aware quotes, schedule bays, draft inspection explanations, trigger maintenance reminders, assist inventory ordering, summarize store performance, and reduce routine bookkeeping effort. It can support diagnostics and technician documentation, but mounting, balancing, inspection, driving, material handling, and most repairs remain physical and safety-sensitive.
narrative.value_capture: Savings can appear as avoided service-writer and administrative hiring, improved quote conversion, fewer missed calls, better bay use, and tighter stock. Installed-tire pricing is easy to compare and fleet accounts can be rebid, so some benefit will migrate to customers; convenience, speed, and constrained local service capacity preserve a meaningful retained share.
narrative.firm_availability: Employer tire and auto-service businesses do transact, and aging owners create a transfer pipeline. Still, many establishments are chain-controlled, too small, franchise-constrained, owner-dependent, or predominantly product retail, and the supplied dataset has no defensible LMM firm-count band, making absolute availability the central unresolved issue.
narrative.demand_durability: Replacement tire shipments remain broadly stable, and every mainstream vehicle powertrain continues to require tires, inspection, and installation. Online tire shopping may shift customer acquisition and product margin, but the physical last mile of fitment and roadworthiness remains local; channel loss rather than disappearance of tire need is the main demand risk.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest evidence gaps are 441340-specific occupation weights, a true LMM firm denominator, transfer completion rates, and measured post-deployment labor outcomes. Competitive price transparency, franchise rules, technician shortages, product liability, inconsistent point-of-sale data, customer resistance to automated advice, and consolidation by large chains could each weaken the thesis.
narrative.principal_driver: Durable replacement demand paired with repeatable customer-intake, scheduling, quoting, inventory, and administrative workflows across multi-location operators.
narrative.principal_weakness: No defensible LMM firm-count estimate, compounded by a physical labor core that limits the share of wages AI can substitute.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 441340 Tire Dealers
sources.supports: Defines the industry as retailers of new or used tires and tubes, including new-tire retail combined with automotive repair, and identifies excluded retreading and wholesale activities.

sources.title: BLS Data Tables for OEWS Industry Charts, May 2024
sources.supports: Reports the largest occupations and employment counts in NAICS 441300, including parts salespersons, tire repairers/changers, drivers, retail salespersons, technicians, supervisors, and managers.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Requirements Survey: Retail Salespersons
sources.supports: Documents in-person and physical requirements: speaking for more than 99.5% of workers, low-posture work for 85.1%, and routine telework for less than 5%.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Requirements Survey: Installation, Maintenance, and Repair Workers
sources.supports: Reports that work was controlled by people for 88.9% of these workers in 2025 and routine telework was allowed for less than 0.5%.

sources.title: Census Bureau: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports December 2025-May 2026 BTOS adoption evidence, including approximately 14% current and 17% expected AI use in Retail Trade and lower adoption among the smallest firms.

sources.title: USTMA February 2026 Forecast Predicts Slightly Higher 2026 Tire Shipments
sources.supports: Projects 338.9 million total U.S. tire shipments in 2026 versus 336.3 million in 2025 and 332.7 million in 2019, with modest growth in replacement categories.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports 2024 survey evidence on five-year plans: 14% of all primary owners, 17% of owners age 55 or older, and 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership.

sources.title: BizBuySell 2025 Fourth Quarter Insight Report
sources.supports: Reports 247 completed Auto Repair and Service Shop sales in 2025 and broader seller and broker evidence on retirement-driven transaction supply; the category is adjacent rather than tire-dealer-specific.
